title: Zhuangzi and the case against meritocracy | Aeon Essays
gist: Drawing on the Daoist Zhuangzi's critique of Confucian meritocracy, the essay argues that 'merit' is largely a social product and that authority manufactures the appearance of virtue rather than reflecting it, undermining the moral justification for inequality that underpins neoliberal markets.

insights.claim: Meritocracy holds that social and economic inequality is just when it reflects individual effort or talent, an ideology that fits neoliberal free-market democracies presenting themselves as open opportunity systems.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Names the load-bearing belief that makes market inequality politically tolerable; weakening it has implications for redistribution politics and consumer/worker sentiment.
insights.quote: Meritocracy implies that inequality is just and fair. Those who rise deserve to rise; those who fall behind are encouraged to try harder.
insights.locator: This is the ideal of meritocracy

insights.claim: The word 'meritocracy' was coined by Michael Young in the 1950s, but Confucianism justified inequality as deserved consequence of individual effort more than two millennia earlier.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Frames meritocracy as an ancient, recurring ideological structure rather than a modern invention, suggesting its persistence is deep-rooted.
insights.quote: ‘Meritocracy’ itself might be a relatively new word, dating back to Michael Young’s coinage in the 1950s

insights.claim: Confucianism translates equal moral capacity at birth into justified unequal outcomes via 'worthiness' (xian), holding economic and political inequality legitimate when it mirrors moral inequality earned through cultivation.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Exposes the mechanism (equal start, unequal effort, deserved outcome) that any meritocratic legitimation reuses, including modern productivity discourse.
insights.quote: Confucianism holds that economic and political inequality are justified when they mirror moral inequality.

insights.claim: The Confucian moral grammar persists in modern self-improvement and hard-work rhetoric, with vocabulary shifting from virtue to productivity and cultivation to performance.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Connects ancient ideology directly to contemporary hustle/performance culture, useful for reading labor-market and self-optimization narratives.
insights.quote: The vocabulary might shift from virtue to productivity and from cultivation to performance, but the moral grammar is still strikingly similar.
insights.locator: This structure was laid out more than

insights.claim: Zhuangzi argues a system ranking people by virtue necessarily creates incentives for self-exploitation, turning cultivation into 'moral capital' that demands extraction from the self.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Reframes burnout and 'rise and grind' culture as a structural output of measurable-merit systems, not individual failing.
insights.quote: A system that ranks people by virtue necessarily creates incentives for self-exploitation.

insights.claim: Zhuangzi explicitly maps Confucian moral self-sacrifice onto modern neoliberal 'hustle culture' where it is trendy to 'rise and grind' and forego basic necessities.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Direct ancient-to-modern bridge for interpreting the self-extraction dynamics in contemporary work and gig culture.
insights.quote: one can see this kind of self-extraction in our modern neoliberal ‘hustle culture’ even more clearly, where it is trendy to ‘rise and grind’ and forego basic necessities

insights.claim: Zhuangzi holds that authority determines who gets counted as worthy rather than the reverse, so power produces the appearance of virtue and hierarchy manufactures its own moral narrative.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: A framework for discounting post-hoc 'they earned it' narratives about winners; success gets retroactively reframed as deserved.
insights.quote: authority determines who gets counted as worthy

insights.claim: Accountability scales inversely with power: a small thief gets arrested while a great thief becomes a ruler, with the great thief defined as one powerful enough that his taking sets the rules.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: A durable lens on selective enforcement and impunity at the top of power structures.
insights.quote: a small thief gets arrested; a great thief becomes a ruler
insights.locator: This does not mean that nothing is right

insights.claim: The 2008 financial crisis destroyed an estimated $50 trillion in global wealth and drove nearly 10 million American families from their homes, yet only one mid-level banker was imprisoned (30 months), illustrating power tipping the scales of accountability.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Concrete impunity data point contrasting petty-theft life sentences with near-zero elite accountability, a recurring systemic-risk and governance theme.
insights.quote: the culprits of the 2008 financial crisis destroyed an estimated $50 trillion in global wealth and drove nearly 10 million American families from their homes. Only one mid-level banker went to prison
insights.locator: We see this play out very often

insights.claim: The capacities that make agency possible (education, health, time, stability, networks) are unevenly distributed before any cultivation begins, so 'virtue' can only be defined by those who already succeeded.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Undercuts 'equality of opportunity' framing; the starting line is itself a field of unequal conditions, relevant to mobility and inequality debates.
insights.quote: the very capacities that make agency possible (such as education, health, time, stability, personal networks) are unevenly distributed before any cultivation can even begin
insights.locator: Yet moral evaluation and its social

insights.claim: Zhuangzi's concept of ziran ('so-of-itself') holds action arises from convergence of conditions rather than a sovereign self, making 'merit' largely a social product and removing the moral engine of inequality.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: If outcomes are co-produced by circumstance, the desert-based defense of inequality collapses, the essay's core argument.
insights.quote: Without a self that can fully own its outcomes, social and political inequality loses its moral engine.

insights.claim: The Liezi parable (riding the wind but still dependent on it) models how apparent self-propelled success is always carried by social, material and historical currents with no individual author.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: A memorable mental model: 'the meritocratic subject mistakes a favourable wind for personal flight' — applicable to attributing investment or career outcomes to skill vs luck.
insights.quote: The meritocratic subject mistakes a favourable wind for personal flight.
insights.locator: In the first chapter of the Zhuangzi

insights.claim: Meritocracy's staying power is psychological: having invested years to improve, people resist questioning the framework because doing so means admitting much of their investment may never pay off.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Explains why belief in meritocracy is sticky even among those it disadvantages, a behavioral driver of political and consumer attitudes.
insights.quote: Once we have poured years of work into becoming better, questioning the framework that evaluates us becomes costly.

insights.claim: A post-desert society oriented by ziran would attend to the conditions that make flourishing possible (institutional support, care, removal of structural disadvantage) rather than sorting individuals by achievement.
insights.speaker: Christine Abigail L Tan
insights.so_what: Sketches the policy orientation (structural support over individual sorting) that follows from rejecting meritocratic desert.
insights.quote: instead of sorting individuals according to the amount of flourishing they’ve achieved, we would attend to the conditions that make flourishing possible
insights.locator: Zhuangzi invites us to resist this story
